title: Hello planisphere
text: Earth sphere (precisely, geoid), three-dimensional object floating space. Yet maps use every day flat: exist two dimensions, paper screen. move curved reality flat representation, need transform coordinates expressed globe latitude longitude Cartesian coordinates plane. simple flattening operation: relies mathematical transformation known map projection, converts curved surface Earth flat map. Projections generally classified according geometric surface used intermediate step : cylindrical, conic, azimuthal, offering different way “wrapping” globe unfolding . However, transformation inevitably introduces distortions. way, similar trying press peel orange onto flat table. matter carefully proceed, surface lie flat without consequences: stretch places, compress others, may even tear fold forced. fundamental constraint applies Earth’s surface projected onto plane. projection can preserve geometric properties , meaning distances, areas, shapes, directions always altered degree, depending chosen projection purpose.

title: Hello planisphere
text: Earth perfect sphere; geodesy accurately approximated ellipsoid. refinement accounts slight flattening poles provides mathematical foundation high-precision geospatial computations. However, planisphere package, adopt spherical model Earth simplicity consistency visual geometric goals. need work directly ellipsoidal models geodesic accuracy, tools GDAL PROJ appropriate, operate ellipsoidal Earth representations.

title: Hello planisphere
text: hood, package relies set JavaScript libraries D3 ecosystem. First, d3-geo provides fundamental building blocks spherical geometry, including coordinate transforms set standard map projections. d3-geo-projection extends system wider collection projection methods, including discontinuous projections. Finally, d3-geo-polygon focuses spherical polygon operations: introduces additional projections rely polygon clipping. https://d3js.org/d3-geo/projection https://github.com/d3/d3-geo-projection https://github.com/d3/d3-geo-polygon

title: Hello planisphere
code: # install.packages("remotes") # remotes::install_github("riatelab/planisphere") library(planisphere) ## V8 engine 12.4.254.21 initialized ## Loading D3.js geospatial stack ## ✔ d3 (7.9.0) ## ✔ d3-geo-polygon (2.0.1) ## ✔ d3-geo-projection (4.0.0) ## ✔ d3-geo (3.1.1) ## ✔ additional script (spilhaus.js) ## Planisphere is ready 🌐 # Data Loading library(sf) ## Linking to GEOS 3.12.1, GDAL 3.8.4, PROJ 9.4.0; sf_use_s2() is TRUE world <- st_read(   system.file("gpkg/land.gpkg", package = "planisphere"),   quiet = TRUE ) result <- planisphere::project(x = world, proj = "geoInterruptedMollweide") planisphere::display(result) result <- planisphere::project(x = world, proj = "geoIcosahedral") |>    display()

title: Hello planisphere
text: Peirce projection   Peters (upside )   projection: d3.geoCylindricalEqualArea().parallel(45).rotate([167, 180]), Hao
code: peirceN <- planisphere::project(                             x = world,                             proj = "PeirceQuincuncial",                             rotate = c(-25, -90),                             additional_layers = TRUE                             ) display(peirceN) peirceS <- planisphere::project(                             x = world,                             proj = "PeirceQuincuncial",                             rotate = c(-25, 90, 180),                             additional_layers = TRUE                             ) display(peirceS) peters <- planisphere::project(                             x = world,                             proj = "geoCylindricalEqualArea",                             parallel = 45,                             rotate = c(167, 180),                             additional_layers = TRUE                             ) display(peters) polar <- planisphere::project(                             x = world,                             proj = "geoAzimuthalEquidistant",                             clipAngle = 150,                             rotate = c(0, -90),                              additional_layers = TRUE                             ) display(polar) hao <- planisphere::project(                             x = world,                             proj = "geoHufnagel",                             a = 0.8,                             b = 0.35,                             psiMax = 50,                             ratio = 1.6,                             angle = 90,                             rotate = c(110, -200, 90),                             additional_layers = TRUE                             ) display(hao)

title: Map projections
text: Earth sphere (precisely, geoid), three-dimensional object floating space. Yet maps use every day flat: exist two dimensions, paper screen. move curved reality flat representation, need transform coordinates expressed globe latitude longitude Cartesian coordinates plane. simple flattening operation: relies mathematical transformation known map projection. Projections generally classified according geometric surface used intermediate step : cylindrical, conic, azimuthal, offering different way “wrapping” globe unfolding .  Beyond classical geometric constructions, also family pseudoprojections, based strict developable surface instead defined analytically reduce balance distortions. category includes pseudocylindrical, pseudoconical, pseudoazimuthal projections, relax geometric constraints order produce visually statistically balanced representations world. Closely related discontinuous projections, introduce intentional breaks cuts map (typically along selected meridians regions) order manage redistribute distortion, improving representation key areas expense global continuity. Finally, approaches rely piecewise mathematical constructions, polyhedral projections, globe first mapped onto polyhedron (e.g., icosahedron cube) unfolded plane.  However, map projections inevitably introduces distortions. way, similar trying press peel orange onto flat table. matter carefully proceed, surface lie flat without consequences: stretch places, compress others, may even tear fold forced. fundamental constraint applies Earth’s surface projected onto plane. projection can preserve geometric properties , meaning distances, areas, shapes, directions always altered degree, depending chosen projection purpose. distortions can visualized using Tissot’s indicatrix, diagnostic tool represents infinitesimal circles globe transformed ellipses map, making possible assess locally projection deforms angles, areas, shapes across space.

title: Map projections
text: Earth perfect sphere; geodesy accurately approximated ellipsoid. refinement accounts slight flattening poles provides mathematical foundation high-precision geospatial computations. However, planisphere package, adopt spherical model Earth simplicity consistency visual geometric goals. sufficient producing maps global scale. need work directly ellipsoidal models geodesic accuracy, tools GDAL PROJ appropriate, operate ellipsoidal Earth representations.

title: Map projections
text: planisphere package provides one hundred map projections. grouped different categories: “cylindrical”, “conic”, “azimuthal”, “perspective”, “pseudocylindrical”, “interrupted”, “polyhedral”, “quincuncial”, “regional”, “pseudoazimuthal”, “miscellaneous”. can retrieve names visualize using registry() gallery() functions. Let’s take look available projections.

title: Map projections
text: main function package project(), can used project spatial data frame. Let’s load world basemap. can now project . additional_layers parameter allows retrieve, together projected basemap, sphere graticules. can visualize whole set using display() function.
code: library(sf) world <- st_read(   system.file("gpkg/land.gpkg", package = "planisphere"),   quiet = TRUE ) spilhaus <- planisphere::project(world, proj = "Spilhaus",                                  additional_layers = TRUE) planisphere::display(spilhaus,  title = "Spilhaus")

title: Map projections
text: Projection functions accept many parameters allow projections customized. examples possible. Peirce Quincuncial Charles Sanders Peirce developed quincuncial projection 1879. name refers way four quadrants world arranged square around central hemisphere. standard form, projection centered North Pole, gives greater emphasis Northern Hemisphere.  Gall-Peters Gall–Peters projection, popularized 1973 Arno Peters originally based work James Gall 19th century, equal-area cylindrical map projection preserves relative surface areas significantly distorting shapes. often contrasted Mercator projection (1569), preserves angles exaggerates size high-latitude regions. “inverted” versions, map flipped along north–south axis, placing Southern Hemisphere top. alter mathematical properties projection, challenges conventional map orientation symbolic hierarchy embedded standard world representations.  Polar projection polar projection, popularized mid-20th century Richard Edes Harrison 1940s, represents world top-view centered North Pole. highlights continuity landmasses oceans around Arctic rather usual east–west framing. type projection widely disseminated United Nations emblem, adopted 1945, based azimuthal equidistant projection centered North Pole, symbolizing world without dominant center.  Hao Xiaoguang projection Hao Xiaoguang Chinese cartographer known developing modern equal-area world map projections aim preserve area relationships reducing visual distortion. work part contemporary approach alternative world map design, seeking balanced representations less centered Euro-American cartographic conventions.
code: peirceN <- planisphere::project(                             x = world,                             proj = "PeirceQuincuncial",                             rotate = c(-25, -90),                             additional_layers = TRUE                             )  peirceS <- planisphere::project(                             x = world,                             proj = "PeirceQuincuncial",                             rotate = c(-25, 90, 180),                             additional_layers = TRUE                             ) peters <- planisphere::project(                             x = world,                             proj = "CylindricalEqualArea",                             parallel = 45,                             rotate = c(167, 180),                             additional_layers = TRUE                             ) polar <- planisphere::project(                             x = world,                             proj = "geoAzimuthalEquidistant",                             clipAngle = 150,                             rotate = c(0, -90),                             additional_layers = TRUE                             ) hao <- planisphere::project(                             x = world,                             proj = "geoHufnagel",                             a = 0.8,                             b = 0.35,                             psiMax = 50,                             ratio = 1.6,                             angle = 90,                             rotate = c(110, -200, 90),                             additional_layers = TRUE                             )

title: Map projections
text: spherical model less accurate geodesic model. Nevertheless, can still provide fairly good approximation standard map projections. Let’s look two geographic areas: France Europe. First, let’s retrieve data online. Europe (LAEA) Lambert azimuthal equal-area (LAEA) projection azimuthal projection centered point located heart Europe, officially defined around (10°E, 52°N). center, directions preserved, areas represented accurately. official projection used Europe. planisphere, projection can reproduced using “AzimuthalEqualArea” projection adjusting rotate parameter. France (Lambert 93) Lambert 93 projection conformal conic projection officially used France cartographic geospatial applications. planisphere, can reproduced using conic projection specifying appropriate parallels center parameters metropolitan France. ConicConformal function produce artifacts outside area interest, clipExtent parameter used properly clip resulting map. Please refer d3.js documentation understand mechanism works. Et voilà!
code: europe <- sf::st_read("https://gisco-services.ec.europa.eu/distribution/v2/nuts/gpkg/NUTS_RG_20M_2024_4326_LEVL_0.gpkg", quiet = TRUE) |>  sf::st_crop(xmin=-10, xmax=45, ymin=25, ymax=72) france <- europe[europe$ISO3_CODE == "FRA",] laea = planisphere::project(europe,                              proj = "AzimuthalEqualArea",                              rotate = c(-10, -52)                             ) lambert93 <- planisphere::project(france,                                   proj = "ConicConformal",                                   rotate = c(-3,0),                                   center = c(0, 46.5),                                  parallels = c(44,49),                                  clipExtent =  list(c(0, 0), c(1000, 1000))                                  )

title: Authors and Citation
text: Nicolas Lambert. Author, maintainer.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Lambert N (2026). planisphere: Map projections. R package version 0.1.0, https://riatelab.github.io/planisphere/.
code: @Manual{,   title = {planisphere: Map projections},   author = {Nicolas Lambert},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.1.0},   url = {https://riatelab.github.io/planisphere/}, }

title: Map projections
text: Map projections package provides access wide range map projections. allows spatial data frames containing geographic coordinates (latitude/longitude) projected. Projection calculations performed using spherical geometry rather ellipsoidal geodetic models.

title: Map projections
text: can install released version planisphere CRAN : Alternatively, can install development version planisphere r-universe :
code: install.packages("planisphere") install.packages("planisphere", repos = c("https://riatelab.r-universe.dev", "https://cloud.r-project.org"))

title: Map projections
text: package provides two main functions. project() applies map projection spatial dataframe. display() plot projected spatial dataframe.   additional_layers = TRUE, can retrieve, along projected basemap, list containing basemap well sphere graticule layers. previoulsy, can visualize directly using display() function.  can customize projections using dedicated parameters. example, obtain polar projection:
code: library(sf)  world <- st_read(   system.file("gpkg/land.gpkg", package = "planisphere"),   quiet = TRUE ) equal <- planisphere::project(x = world, proj = "EqualEarth") planisphere::display(equal) imago <- planisphere::project(x = world, proj = "Imago") planisphere::display(imago) mollweide <- planisphere::project(x = world,                                   proj = "InterruptedMollweide",                                   additional_layers = TRUE                                   ) planisphere::display(mollweide) polar <- planisphere::project(x = world,                                   proj = "AzimuthalEquidistant",                                   clipAngle = 150,                                   rotate = c(0, -90),                                   additional_layers = TRUE                                   ) planisphere::display(polar)

title: Map projections
text: package provides hundred map projections. retrieve names, can use registry() function. gallery() function, can quickly visualize set projections glance. default, function displays 12 randomly selected projections. example.

title: Map projections
text: hood, package executes JavaScript code. built V8 engine, using context default libraries preloaded. However, can also create new context load additional libraries using new_v8_context() function. load, package exposes projection functions provided three JavaScript libraries D3 ecosystem. grateful Mike Bostock, Philippe Rivière, Jason Davies, Ricky Reusser, Charles Karney, contributors helped develop maintain powerful geospatial tools. d3-geo: https://d3js.org/d3-geo/projection d3-geo-projection: https://github.com/d3/d3-geo-projection d3-geo-polygon: https://github.com/d3/d3-geo-polygon addition, custom script Spilhaus projection added (thanks Torben Jansen). details available projections parameters, please refer documentation libraries. interesting can directly pass D3 function parameters. example equivalent approach can much convenient already familiar D3 ecosystem used working .
code: planisphere::projection(x = world,                          proj = "AzimuthalEqualArea",                         rotate = c(-10, -52)                         ) planisphere::projection(x = world,                          proj = "d3.geoAzimuthalEqualArea().rotate([-10, 52])"                         )

title: Map projections
text: One can contribute package pull requests report issues ask questions

what: Build a D3.js projection chain — build_projection_chain
title: Build a D3.js projection chain — build_projection_chain
text: Internal helper constructs JavaScript D3 projection call string.

title: Build a D3.js projection chain — build_projection_chain
code: build_projection_chain(proj = "geoSomething", verbose = TRUE, ...)

title: Build a D3.js projection chain — build_projection_chain
text: proj Character. Name D3 projection (without geo prefix). verbose Logical. TRUE, prints generated projection chain. ... Additional projection parameters passed method calls (e.g. rotate, center, scale, reflectX).

title: Build a D3.js projection chain — build_projection_chain
text: character string representing D3.js projection chain.

title: Build a D3.js projection chain — build_projection_chain
text: function translates R projection parameters valid D3.js projection constructor chain (e.g. d3.geoOrthographic().rotate([...])). used internally project() generate projection expression evaluated inside V8 JavaScript context.

title: Display a projected planisphere — display
text: Render complete planisphere projected object created project(). display includes projected sphere outline, graticule, geographic features.

title: Display a projected planisphere — display
code: display(x, title = NULL, extent = NULL)

title: Display a projected planisphere — display
text: x list returned project(), containing three sf objects: basemap: projected geographic features sphere: outline projected globe graticule: projected latitude/longitude grid title Character string. Optional title add plot. Default NULL. extent object. spatial extent used clip define mapping area (`sf`, `sfc`, `bbox`).

title: Display a projected planisphere — display
text: base R plot.

title: Display a projected planisphere — display
text: visualization follows D3.js spherical projection model used package. rendering order : 1. Sphere background 2. Graticule 3. Projected landmass (basemap) 4. Sphere outline geometries expected already projected spherical coordinates. function intended fast visualization exploration D3-based projections.

title: Display a projected planisphere — display
code: library(sf) #> Linking to GEOS 3.12.1, GDAL 3.8.4, PROJ 9.4.0; sf_use_s2() is TRUE world <- st_read(   system.file("gpkg/land.gpkg", package = "planisphere"),   quiet = TRUE )  result <- planisphere::project(   x = world,   proj = "Eckert1",   additional_layers = TRUE ) planisphere::display(result)

what: Flip geometry along Y axis — flipY
title: Flip geometry along Y axis — flipY
text: Internal helper vertically flips geometries multiplying Y coordinates -1.

title: Flip geometry along Y axis — flipY
code: flipY(x)

title: Flip geometry along Y axis — flipY
text: x sf object.

title: Flip geometry along Y axis — flipY
text: sf object flipped Y coordinates.

title: Flip geometry along Y axis — flipY
text: function used display normalization D3-based spherical projections, coordinate systems may require inversion Y axis correct rendering base R plotting. operates directly geometry slot sf object.

what: Display a gallery of map projections — gallery
title: Display a gallery of map projections — gallery
text: Creates gallery projected world maps using projections available planisphere. default, random sample projections selected projection registry displayed multi-panel layout.

title: Display a gallery of map projections — gallery
code: gallery(   projections = NULL,   sample = 12,   ncol = 4,   title = TRUE,   verbose = TRUE )

title: Display a gallery of map projections — gallery
text: projections Character vector projection names. `NULL`, projections selected `registry()`. sample Integer. Number projections display `projections = NULL`. Ignored `projections` provided. Set `NULL` display available projections. ncol Integer. Number columns gallery layout. title Logical. projection names displayed titles? verbose Logical. Display progress messages rendering projections.

title: Display a gallery of map projections — gallery
text: Draws gallery projected maps current graphics device returns `NULL` invisibly.

title: Display a gallery of map projections — gallery
text: function uses bundled world land dataset applies selected projection [project()]. Maps displayed using [display()] base R graphics layout.

title: Display a gallery of map projections — gallery
code: # Display a random sample of 12 projections gallery(verbose = FALSE)   # Display selected projections gallery(   projections = c(     "Mercator",     "Robinson",     "Orthographic",     "EqualEarth"   ),   ncol = 2,   verbose = FALSE )

title: Initialize D3.js projection system — init
text: Create V8 JavaScript context load required D3.js libraries enable cartographic projections.

title: Initialize D3.js projection system — init
code: init(   libs = c("https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/d3@7", "https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/d3-geo@3",     "https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/d3-geo-projection@4",     "https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/d3-geo-polygon@1"),   verbose = TRUE )

title: Initialize D3.js projection system — init
text: libs Character vector JavaScript library URLs load. default, libraries loaded jsDelivr CDN, URLs may replaced local files alternative CDNs. verbose Logical. TRUE, prints status loaded library console.

title: Initialize D3.js projection system — init
text: V8 JavaScript context specified libraries loaded.

title: Initialize D3.js projection system — init
text: function initializes V8 JavaScript runtime loads D3.js geospatial ecosystem used package, including: - d3 (core library) - d3-geo (spherical geometry projections) - d3-geo-projection (extended map projections) - d3-geo-polygon (polygon clipping sphere) Libraries loaded jsDelivr CDN default, local custom URLs can provided. projections package computed using D3.js spherical geometry, Earth modeled sphere rather ellipsoid.

title: Initialize D3.js projection system — init
code: ct <- planisphere::init() #> Loading JavaScript libraries #> ✔ https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/d3@7 #> ✔ https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/d3-geo@3 #> ✔ https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/d3-geo-projection@4 #> ✔ https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/d3-geo-polygon@1

title: New V8 context — new_v8_context
text: Create new V8 JavaScript context preloaded D3.js geospatial stack.

title: New V8 context — new_v8_context
code: new_v8_context(   libs = c("https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/d3@7", "https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/d3-geo@3",     "https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/d3-geo-polygon@2",     "https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/d3-geo-projection@4"),   verbose = TRUE )

title: New V8 context — new_v8_context
text: libs Character vector JavaScript library URLs load. default, libraries loaded jsDelivr CDN. Users may replace local files, pinned versions, alternative CDNs. verbose Logical. TRUE, prints loading status library.

title: New V8 context — new_v8_context
text: V8 JavaScript context D3.js geospatial stack loaded ready cartographic computations.

title: New V8 context — new_v8_context
text: function initializes fresh V8 JavaScript runtime automatically loads latest versions required D3.js libraries jsDelivr CDN. following JavaScript libraries loaded default: - d3 (core library): https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/d3 - d3-geo (spherical geometry geographic projections): https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/d3-geo - d3-geo-projection (extended cartographic projections): https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/d3-geo-projection - d3-geo-polygon (spherical polygon clipping): https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/d3-geo-polygon libraries fetched dynamically runtime jsDelivr CDN, ensuring recent compatible versions used unless explicitly overridden via `libs` argument. projections computed environment rely D3.js spherical geometry, Earth modeled sphere rather ellipsoid. allows fast consistent computation map projections geometric transformations.

title: New V8 context — new_v8_context
code: ct <- planisphere::new_v8_context() #> V8 engine 12.4.254.21 initialized #> Loading additional JavaScript libraries #> [OK] https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/d3@7 #> [OK] https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/d3-geo@3 #> [OK] https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/d3-geo-polygon@2 #> [OK] https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/d3-geo-projection@4 #> Planisphere is ready # Then: library(sf) world <- st_read(   system.file("gpkg/land.gpkg", package = "planisphere"),   quiet = TRUE ) out <- planisphere::project(x = world, proj = "InterruptedBoggs", ct = ct)

title: Package description — planisphere
text: wrapper around D3.js cartographic projections design visualize world maps (planispheres). Projections computed using D3.js spherical geometry rather ellipsoidal geodesic models.

title: Package description — planisphere
text: Maintainer: Nicolas Lambert nicolas.lambert@cnrs.fr (ORCID) Authors: Nicolas Lambert nicolas.lambert@cnrs.fr (ORCID)

what: Project geographic data using a D3.js projection — project
title: Project geographic data using a D3.js projection — project
text: Projects spatial data using D3.js geographic projections executed V8 JavaScript context. projection performed spherical coordinates, following D3.js design principles, Earth modeled sphere rather ellipsoid.

title: Project geographic data using a D3.js projection — project
code: project(   x,   proj = "geoAzimuthalEqualArea",   rotate = NULL,   reflectX = NULL,   reflectY = NULL,   scale = 500,   center = NULL,   parallel = NULL,   parallels = NULL,   clipExtent = NULL,   clip = TRUE,   graticule = c(10, 10),   additional_layers = FALSE,   verbose = FALSE,   ct = .planisphere$ct,   ... )

title: Project geographic data using a D3.js projection — project
text: x sf spatial dataframe project. proj D3 projection name constructor (e.g. "geoInterruptedHomolosine"). rotate Rotation parameters passed projection. reflectX Logical; whether reflect projection X axis. reflectY Logical; whether reflect projection Y axis. scale Projection scale factor (D3 spherical scale, planar CRS units). center Optional projection center. parallel Optional standard parallel projection parallels Optional standard parallels projection clipExtent Projection’s viewport clip extent specified bounds pixels clip TRUE, clips projected geometries projected sphere. graticule Numeric vector longitude/latitude step size graticule generation. additional_layers Logical. TRUE, adds graticule sphere layers. case, function returns list. FALSE (default), returns spatial data frame. verbose Logical. Display messages ct custom V8 JavaScript context needed. See new_v8_context(). ... Additional parameters passed projection builder.

title: Project geographic data using a D3.js projection — project
text: additionalLayers = FALSE, single sf object corresponding projected basemap. additionalLayers = TRUE, list sf objects: basemap: projected input geometries sphere: outline projected globe graticule: projected graticule lines

title: Project geographic data using a D3.js projection — project
text: D3 geographic projections based spherical geometry. transformations assume perfect sphere, differs traditional GIS pipelines often rely ellipsoidal datums (e.g., WGS84). result, function primarily intended visualization cartographic rendering rather high-precision geodetic computation.

title: Project geographic data using a D3.js projection — project
code: library(sf) world <- st_read(   system.file("gpkg/land.gpkg", package = "planisphere"),   quiet = TRUE )  result <- planisphere::project(x = world, proj = "InterruptedBoggs")

title: List available map projections — registry
text: Returns list available map projections registered planisphere. Projections organized thematic families facilitate exploration selection. default, projections returned single sorted character vector. Alternatively, specific family projections can requested.

title: List available map projections — registry
code: registry(type = NULL)

title: List available map projections — registry
text: type Character. Optional projection family. Must one : "cylindrical", "conic", "azimuthal", "perspective", "pseudocylindrical", "interrupted", "polyhedral", "quincuncial", "regional", "pseudoazimuthal", "miscellaneous".

title: List available map projections — registry
text: sorted unique character vector projection names. `type` provided, projections belonging selected family returned.

title: List available map projections — registry
code: # List all available projections  # List cylindrical projections registry(type = "cylindrical") #> [1] "CylindricalEqualArea"     "CylindricalStereographic" #> [3] "Equirectangular"          "Mercator"                 #> [5] "Miller"                   "TransverseMercator"        # Explore regional projections registry("regional") #>  [1] "ChamberlinAfrica"            "ModifiedStereographicAlaska" #>  [3] "ModifiedStereographicGs48"   "ModifiedStereographicGs50"   #>  [5] "ModifiedStereographicLee"    "ModifiedStereographicMiller" #>  [7] "Patterson"                   "Robinson"                    #>  [9] "TwoPointAzimuthalUsa"        "TwoPointEquidistantUsa"      #> [11] "Winkel3"                      # Visualize polyhedral projections gallery(projections = registry("polyhedral"), verbose = FALSE)
